ensemble.information_gap: The prior CT reportedly showed a 'possible duodenal diverticulum' but the anatomic detail, relationship to the biliary system, and any associated inflammatory changes were apparently inconclusive; additionally, the gastric band's relationship to surrounding structures and any intra-abdominal pathology explaining the postprandial RUQ-to-back pain pattern has not been fully characterized with contrast enhancement.
ensemble.expected_finding: Confirmation or exclusion of a duodenal diverticulum and its relationship to the common bile duct/ampulla (Lemmel syndrome), evidence of cholecystitis or biliary ductal dilation not captured on ultrasound, gastric band-related complications (slippage, erosion, peri-band inflammation), or another structural cause of the postprandial RUQ and back pain pattern.
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The prior non-contrast or limited CT identified only a 'possible' duodenal diverticulum without definitive characterization, and a contrast-enhanced CT abdomen is the appropriate next step to better delineate biliary, duodenal, and band-related anatomy given persistent unexplained symptoms.

A contrast-enhanced CT abdomen is ordered to definitively characterize the duodenal diverticulum, assess the biliary tree and pancreatic head in detail, evaluate for any gastric band complication not visible on plain films, and identify any other intra-abdominal source of his symptoms that the prior non-contrast or limited study may have missed.

verification.actual_finding: The CT with IV contrast showed a normal-appearing biliary system, gallbladder, and pancreas, a gastric band without malposition, and an 18x19 mm lesion medial to the third duodenum/lateral to the pancreatic head likely representing a prominent pancreatic lobule rather than a duodenal diverticulum.
verification.relation_to_expectation: The study failed to confirm a duodenal diverticulum (the 'possible' prior finding was reinterpreted as a pancreatic lobule), found no biliary ductal dilation, no cholecystitis, and no gastric band complication, leaving the structural cause of the patient's postprandial RUQ-to-back pain unexplained.

steps.ordered: Ultrasound Abdomen (LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN))

ensemble.other_hypothesis: Biliary colic / choledocholithiasis / biliary dyskinesia; possible periampullary or duodenal pathology (CT noted 18x19mm lesion medial to third portion of duodenum / lateral to pancreatic head described as 'likely prominent pancreatic lobule')
ensemble.information_gap: The prior RUQ ultrasound confirmed gallstones but the physician needs dynamic, real-time assessment of the gallbladder wall, pericholecystic fluid, common bile duct diameter, and sonographic Murphy's sign to determine whether the gallstones are causing cholecystitis or biliary obstruction — findings that CT with contrast and a prior static US may not have fully characterized.
ensemble.expected_finding: Gallbladder wall thickening (>3mm), pericholecystic fluid, positive sonographic Murphy's sign, or a dilated common bile duct suggesting biliary obstruction or acute cholecystitis; alternatively, a normal gallbladder that would shift focus toward the duodenal/pancreatic lesion seen on CT.
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The order is clinically logical given the known gallstones and postprandial RUQ pain pattern, but the history already documents a prior RUQ ultrasound showing gallstones and the CT showed a normal-appearing gallbladder, so the incremental yield of a repeat focused gallbladder ultrasound is moderate rather than high — it may add real-time functional assessment (sonographic Murphy's, CBD measurement) that the prior studies lacked.
grounding: History: 'ER work up has included US of RUQ which shows gallstones'
History: '2 month hx of RUQ pain radiating to his epigastrum and back after fatty food intake'
History: 'Lab work up not consistent with acute infection or common bile duct stone'
CT Abdomen: 'The liver, gallbladder, spleen, pancreas... appear normal'
CT Abdomen: 'An 18 x19 mm rounded lesion is noted medial to the third portion of the duodenum and lateral to the pancreatic head... This likely represents a prominent pancreatic lobule'
Labs: Bilirubin, Total: 0.4 mg/dL (ref 0.0-1.5) — normal
Labs: Alkaline Phosphatase: 74.0 IU/L (ref 39.0-117.0) — normal
Labs: ALT: 21.0, AST: 21.0 — normal liver enzymes
Labs: Lipase: 53.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0) — upper normal range
Labs: Amylase: 107.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-100.0) — mildly elevated
ensemble.reasoning: This patient has a 2-month history of postprandial RUQ pain radiating to the epigastrium and back — a classic biliary colic pattern — with confirmed gallstones on prior ultrasound. Although labs do not suggest cholangitis or CBD stone and CT shows a grossly normal gallbladder, the physician is ordering a dedicated gallbladder ultrasound to obtain real-time functional data (sonographic Murphy's sign, gallbladder wall thickness, pericholecystic fluid, CBD diameter) that the prior studies may not have captured, in order to determine whether the gallstones are the culprit and whether cholecystectomy is indicated. The mildly elevated amylase and the indeterminate periduodenal lesion on CT add secondary motivation to ensure the biliary tree and pancreatic head region are adequately characterized.

representative_ex_ante.other_hypothesis: Biliary colic / choledocholithiasis / biliary dyskinesia; possible periampullary or duodenal pathology (CT noted 18x19mm lesion medial to third portion of duodenum / lateral to pancreatic head described as 'likely prominent pancreatic lobule')
representative_ex_ante.information_gap: The prior RUQ ultrasound confirmed gallstones but the physician needs dynamic, real-time assessment of the gallbladder wall, pericholecystic fluid, common bile duct diameter, and sonographic Murphy's sign to determine whether the gallstones are causing cholecystitis or biliary obstruction — findings that CT with contrast and a prior static US may not have fully characterized.

verification.actual_finding: Innumerable small gallstones are present but the gallbladder wall thickening (5 mm) is attributed to periportal fat rather than true edema, with no pericholecystic fluid, no sonographic Murphy's sign, and a normal common bile duct (4 mm), arguing against acute cholecystitis or biliary obstruction.
verification.relation_to_expectation: The physician expected signs of cholecystitis or biliary obstruction (wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, positive Murphy's sign, or CBD dilation), but none of these were confirmed, shifting focus away from the gallbladder as the culprit and toward the duodenal/pancreatic lesion seen on CT.
